---
title: "JSC370 Final Project"
author: "Your Name"
jupyter: python3
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: false
---



This is my JSC370 Final Project website. I will showcase a few interactive visuals here.



## First step

First I will load and process the Starbucks data:

```{python}
#| echo: true
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from collections import Counter
import re

# Load data
sb_locs  = pd.read_csv("starbucks-locations.csv")
sb_nutr  = pd.read_csv("starbucks-menu-nutrition.csv", skipinitialspace=True)
usa_pop  = pd.read_csv("us_state_pop.csv")
usa_states = pd.read_csv("states.csv")

# Filter US locations and count stores per state
sb_usa = sb_locs[sb_locs["Country"] == "US"]
sb_locs_state = (
    sb_usa
    .groupby("State/Province")
    .size()
    .reset_index(name="n_stores")
    .rename(columns={"State/Province": "state"})
)

# Merge with population (via state abbreviation)
usa_pop_abbr = usa_pop.merge(usa_states, left_on="state", right_on="State")
sb_locs_state = sb_locs_state.merge(
    usa_pop_abbr, left_on="state", right_on="Abbreviation"
)

# Top 10 words in menu item names
stop_words = {"the", "and", "with", "a", "an", "of", "in", "or", "no", "on"}
word_counts = Counter(
    w for name in sb_nutr["Item"].dropna()
    for w in re.findall(r"[a-zA-Z]+", name.lower())
    if len(w) > 2 and w not in stop_words
)
top10_words = [w for w, _ in word_counts.most_common(10)]

def find_top_word(item_name):
    for w in top10_words:
        if w in str(item_name).lower():
            return w
    return None

sb_nutr["top_word"] = sb_nutr["Item"].apply(find_top_word)
sb_top = sb_nutr.dropna(subset=["top_word"]).copy()
```

<br>

## Second step

Then I will add some code to create the **plotly** figures.

Note: Name each code chunk, like this one: `plot1`

```{python}
#| label: plot1
fig = px.scatter(
    sb_locs_state,
    x="population",
    y="n_stores",
    color="state_x",
    hover_name="state_x",
    title="Starbucks Stores vs. State Population",
    labels={"population": "Population", "n_stores": "Number of Stores", "state_x": "State"},
    template="plotly_white",
    opacity=0.8,
)
fig.show()
```

<br>

## Third step

Create tabs to display each figure.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Histogram of Calories

```{python}
#| label: p1
fig = px.histogram(
    sb_nutr,
    x="Calories",
    color="Category",
    barmode="overlay",
    opacity=0.6,
    nbins=30,
    title="Caloric Distribution of Starbucks Menu Items",
    labels={"Calories": "Calories", "Category": "Category"},
    template="plotly_white",
)
fig.show()
```

### Boxplot of Nutrition

```{python}
#| label: p2
nutrients = ["Calories", "Fat (g)", "Carb. (g)", "Fiber (g)", "Protein (g)"]
sb_top_long = sb_top.melt(
    id_vars=["Item", "top_word"],
    value_vars=nutrients,
    var_name="Nutrient",
    value_name="Value",
)

fig = px.box(
    sb_top_long,
    x="top_word",
    y="Value",
    color="Nutrient",
    title="Nutritional Values of Starbucks Items by Top 10 Words",
    labels={"top_word": "Item Word", "Value": "Nutritional Value"},
    template="plotly_white",
)
fig.update_layout(boxmode="group")
fig.show()
```

:::



Done!
